question: Two cars reach an intersection with no signs or signals at the same time. Who must yield?
choices: The driver on the right yields to the driver on the left first
The driver on the left yields to the driver on the right
Whichever vehicle is traveling faster gets to proceed first
Both drivers stop and then take turns waving each other on
explanation: At an intersection with no stop sign or signal, drivers must yield to vehicles approaching from the right.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 39 — Right of Way at Stop Signs

question: You are turning left at an intersection with a green light. Oncoming traffic is approaching. You must:
choices: Yield to the oncoming cars traveling straight ahead and turn only when the gap is safe
Turn quickly across their path before any of the oncoming cars reach the intersection
Stop in the middle of the intersection and wait there until the light turns red
Honk your horn firmly to claim the right-of-way and complete the left turn anyway
explanation: Drivers turning left must yield to oncoming cars traveling straight ahead. When two vehicles face each other and one is turning left, the one going straight goes first.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 35, 39 — Right of Way (Left Turns)

question: At a 4-WAY or ALL-WAY stop, who goes first?
choices: Whichever driver is on the busier or the wider of the two roads
The largest and heaviest vehicle waiting at the intersection
The driver who is turning left always gets to go ahead of everyone else at the stop
The driver who reached the intersection first, after making a complete stop
explanation: First to stop = first to go. If two arrive at the same time, the vehicle on the right goes first. And when in doubt, let the other traffic go ahead — your safety comes first.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 39-40 — Right of Way at Stop Signs

question: An ambulance approaches with lights and siren on while you are in an intersection. You should:
choices: Stop immediately right where you are and stay there until the ambulance has fully passed you
Speed up and keep on driving to get away from the ambulance as fast as you possibly can
Drive through the intersection first, then pull to the right edge of the road and stop
Move over into the left lane and continue driving along beside the rest of the moving traffic
explanation: Pull to the right edge of the road and stop for emergency vehicles using lights/siren — but if you're in an intersection, go ahead and drive through it before pulling over.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 11 — Move Over Law / Emergency Vehicles

question: You are entering a road from a driveway, alley, or parking lot. Who has the right-of-way?
choices: You do, because a driver who is entering traffic always has the right-of-way
Pedestrians on the sidewalk AND all vehicles already on the main road
Whichever driver or pedestrian happens to be moving faster at the time
Vehicles already on the road have it, but pedestrians on the sidewalk do not
explanation: Drivers crossing a sidewalk from a driveway, alley, or parking lot must yield to pedestrians, and drivers entering a road must yield to vehicles already on it.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 40 — General Right of Way Rules

question: A pedestrian is crossing in a crosswalk. You must:
choices: Yield — a driver must yield to pedestrians in or about to enter a crosswalk
Slow down a little and then carefully steer around them while they cross
Honk your horn at them so that they hurry up and finish crossing the street faster
Proceed through if the pedestrian is still over on the other half of the street
explanation: Drivers must yield to pedestrians in or about to enter a crosswalk — and not all crosswalks are marked. Watch for pedestrians at every intersection.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 34-35 — Crosswalks / Pedestrians

question: When entering a roundabout, you must:
choices: Come to a complete stop before entering the roundabout every single time
Enter quickly because circulating traffic must yield to you
Yield only to traffic that is entering the roundabout from your right side
Yield to traffic in ALL lanes of the roundabout, not just the lane closest to you
explanation: Slow down, and yield to traffic already circulating — in all lanes of the roundabout. Traffic travels counterclockwise around the center island. Arkansas has over 170 roundabouts.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 36-38 — Roundabouts

question: Even with a green light, you should not start across an intersection unless:
choices: The driver who is waiting behind you honks the horn for you to move ahead
You only intend to make a right turn at the intersection just ahead of you
You can get through it without having to stop and block traffic
Traffic ahead of you is heavy and moving slowly through the area
explanation: Drivers may not enter an intersection unless they can get through without stopping. Getting caught when the light changes blocks cross traffic — and you can be ticketed for blocking an intersection.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 40, 61 — General Right of Way / Intersecting Traffic

question: A pedestrian carrying a white cane or using a guide dog is crossing ahead. They have:
choices: Exactly the same right-of-way as any other pedestrian
Absolute right-of-way — and do not use your horn, which could confuse or frighten them
Right-of-way only when they are crossing within a marked crosswalk
No special right-of-way at all, beyond the ordinary rules that already apply to every driver
explanation: Blind pedestrians using a guide dog or white cane have ABSOLUTE right-of-way. Never honk at them — it can confuse or frighten a blind pedestrian.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 35 — Pedestrians

question: You are passing another vehicle when it slows down ahead of you. Who must yield?
choices: The vehicle being passed must speed up to let you complete the pass
Neither vehicle, since passing drivers have the right-of-way
The vehicle being passed must pull over onto the shoulder of the road
You — the passing driver must yield to the vehicle being passed
explanation: Drivers passing a vehicle going the same direction must yield to the vehicle being passed — even if it slows or stops, since it may be turning left or avoiding a hazard you can't see.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 40 — General Right of Way Rules (Passing)

question: Driving on a sidewalk is:
choices: Illegal, except to cross it — and you must yield to pedestrians when crossing
Allowed any time the traffic on the road becomes heavy
Allowed as a place to park whenever no other spots are open
Allowed only for motorcycles, because they take up far less space than larger cars do
explanation: It is illegal to drive on a sidewalk except to cross it (at a driveway or alley), and pedestrians on the sidewalk always have the right-of-way.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 40 — General Right of Way Rules

question: In a two-lane roundabout, to turn LEFT you should generally:
choices: Use the right lane and signal before turning to the left
Use the left lane, unless signs or markings say otherwise
Use either lane you prefer when making a left-hand turn
Avoid roundabouts entirely whenever you turn left
explanation: Like other intersections: left lane to go left, right lane to go right, either lane to go straight unless marked otherwise. Yield to traffic in ALL lanes before entering, and give trucks extra room inside.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 38 — How to Navigate Roundabouts

question: An oncoming car has its turn signal on. You should turn in front of it only when:
choices: You can clearly see that the oncoming car's turn signal is switched on and blinking toward you, which means the turn is coming
The oncoming car appears to still be more than one hundred feet away from where you happen to be sitting and waiting to turn
The driver actually starts the turn — signals can be left on by mistake, and motorcycle signals don't cancel automatically
You are stopped together at a four-way stop and the other driver is clearly signaling a firm intention to make a turn very soon
explanation: Don't trust a blinker alone — the driver may mean a turn beyond you or forgot to cancel the signal. Wait until they actually begin turning.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 61 — Intersecting Traffic

question: Right-of-way rules give a driver:
choices: An absolute right-of-way to proceed without yielding
Clear and complete priority over any pedestrian nearby
The full legal right to proceed straight through an intersection without needing to look for other traffic first
No absolute right-of-way — you must still do everything possible to avoid striking a pedestrian or vehicle
explanation: Traffic law determines who must YIELD — it never grants absolute right-of-way. When in doubt, let the other traffic go ahead; your safety comes first.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 35, 39 — Right of Way

question: Before entering an intersection, you should look left and right, and then:
choices: Look a second time in the direction of oncoming traffic before crossing
Proceed immediately as soon as it appears to be clear
Honk your horn briefly to warn any of the cross traffic
Roll your windows up and turn down the radio before you start to cross over
explanation: Look left, look right — then take a second look in the direction of oncoming traffic before you cross. Intersections include driveways, side streets, and parking-lot entrances.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 48 — Stay Alert (Intersections)

question: You are stopped at an intersection and parked cars block your view of cross traffic. You should:
choices: Accelerate quickly to get across the intersection before any of the cross traffic arrives
Ease forward slowly until you can see, which also lets approaching drivers see you
Honk your horn to warn cross traffic and then proceed through the intersection
Reverse out of the intersection and go find another route around the parked cars and the obstruction
explanation: When your view of a cross street is blocked, ease forward until you can clearly see crossing traffic — moving forward slowly also makes you visible to approaching drivers.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 48 — Stay Alert (Intersections)

question: Before turning right at an intersection, you must check for:
choices: Only the traffic that is approaching the intersection from your left-hand side as you slow to turn
Only the traffic that happens to be following along directly behind your own vehicle as you slow down to make the turn
Traffic approaching from the left, oncoming vehicles turning left into your path, AND pedestrians crossing where you are turning
Nothing at all, because a vehicle making a right turn always has the right-of-way over everyone else at the intersection
explanation: A right turn has three checks: traffic from your left, oncoming cars turning left into the same street, and pedestrians in the crosswalk you're turning across.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 48 — Stay Alert (Intersections)

question: When crossing a wide road, stopping halfway across in the median is safe only when:
choices: Traffic on the road is light and not very many cars are around
You first switch on your hazard lights to warn the other drivers
Never, since you cannot stop in a median
The median divider is large enough for your entire vehicle
explanation: Never stop in a median too small for your vehicle — your car would stick out into the lanes of traffic. Cross only when you can clear the road or fully fit in the median.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 61 — Intersecting Traffic

question: You have the right-of-way, but another driver doesn't yield and keeps coming. You should:
choices: Let them go ahead. 'When in doubt, bail out' — your safety trumps all right-of-way rules
Proceed on through the intersection, because the right-of-way clearly belongs to you here
Stop your car right in the middle of the intersection and wait there for the other driver
Lean on the horn and proceed straight through, since the law is on your side
explanation: If you feel uncomfortable or your safety is threatened, let the other traffic go ahead — even when the rules say you go first. Your safety always comes first.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 39 — Right-of-Way Rules

question: You are preparing to turn right where a bicyclist is riding along the right side of the road. You should:
choices: Merge with the bicycle traffic before the turn rather than turning directly across the cyclist's path
Speed up and turn sharply across the road in front of the cyclist well before they reach the intersection
Honk your horn repeatedly until the cyclist comes to a complete stop and waves you on to make your turn first
Move all the way over and make your right turn from the left lane so that you stay clear of the cyclist
explanation: Don't overtake a cyclist and then cut across their path — that's a common crash. Merge toward the right with the bike traffic, look right and left again, and know the cyclist's speed before turning.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 67 — Sharing the Road (Right Turns)

question: When driving through a roundabout, you should check the crosswalks:
choices: Only before you enter the roundabout, since pedestrians are allowed to cross at the entrance and nowhere else
Only on the occasions when a pedestrian crossing signal is actually present and lit at the roundabout
Never at all, because painted crosswalks are simply not used at roundabout intersections in Arkansas
Both when entering AND when exiting the roundabout — be ready to let pedestrians finish crossing
explanation: Keep looking around in a roundabout: check for pedestrians waiting or crossing on the way in, and look one more time for the crosswalk as you exit.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 38 — How to Navigate Roundabouts

question: Which statement about pedestrians is true?
choices: Pedestrians must always yield the right-of-way to cars in every single situation, whether or not a crosswalk is painted there
Drivers are only ever required to yield to pedestrians at those intersections that happen to have a traffic signal controlling them
Drivers must yield, where necessary, to avoid striking pedestrians who are legally crossing — even if no crosswalk is marked
Pedestrians are simply not permitted to cross the road at an intersection unless it has a clearly marked and painted crosswalk
explanation: Not all crosswalks are marked. Drivers must yield where necessary to avoid striking pedestrians legally crossing the road, and watch for them at every intersection.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 34-35 — Crosswalks / Pedestrians

question: As you approach a roundabout, the FIRST thing you should do is:
choices: Speed up so you can merge smoothly with the circulating traffic
Slow down — going slower gives you more time to yield and makes any crash less severe
Come to a complete stop and wait for the roundabout to empty
Move into the left lane no matter which exit you plan to take
explanation: Navigate a roundabout as easy as 1-2-3: (1) slow down, (2) look around and pick your lane, (3) be ready to yield to traffic already circulating. The curve of a roundabout naturally encourages slower, safer speeds.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 38 — How to Navigate Roundabouts

question: At a four-way stop, you and a car directly across from you stop at the same time. You are turning left; they are going straight. Who goes first?
choices: The car going straight goes first
You do, since you turn left
Whoever honks first
The larger and the heavier of the two cars
explanation: When two vehicles are directly across from each other and one is turning left, the vehicle going straight goes first. (First to stop goes first; if tied, the vehicle on the right goes.)
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 39 — Right of Way at Stop Signs

question: A large truck or bus is circulating in the lane next to you inside a roundabout. You should:
choices: Drive right alongside it so that you stay even with it the whole way through the curve of the roundabout
Speed up and pass it quickly on the inside lane before it reaches its own exit
Come to a complete stop inside the roundabout and patiently wait for the large vehicle to go first and exit
Give it room and avoid driving next to it — large vehicles may need extra space inside the roundabout
explanation: Give large vehicles like trucks and buses extra space in a roundabout and don't drive next to them — they may need more than one lane to get through. Follow the pavement markings to your exit.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 38 — How to Navigate Roundabouts
